title: Home
text:
Welcome to my coursework website for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
This website showcases the visualisations, analysis, and exercises that I have completed throughout the module. It serves as a portfolio of my learning journey in visual analytics using Quarto and R.

title: About
text:
This website was created for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
Its purpose is to document and present the visualisations, analysis, and coursework outputs that I have completed throughout the module. The site acts as a structured portfolio of my work, allowing different exercises and assignments to be organised into separate sections for easy navigation.
The website was built using:

Quarto for website generation
RStudio for development
R for data visualisation and analysis
GitHub for version control
Vercel for deployment

Through this website, I aim to present my coursework in a clear, organised, and accessible format.

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
This exercise looks to explore the fundamentals of visualisations with R.
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_bar
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_dotplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.









Note: y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
The following code below does 2 steps:

scale_y_continuous is used to turn off the y-axis, and
binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_histogram()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









It is to note that the default bin is 30.

Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
The following code chunk does the following:

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  










Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
The following code changes the fill of the histogram by using sub-group of `aesthetic()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_density()
This computes and plots the kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useflu alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








The next code plots 2 kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_boxplot()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








Notches are used in boxplots to help visually assess whether medians of distributions differ. If it does not overlap, it means that the medians are different.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_violin

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_point()
This is to create scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Combinatiation of geom objects
The code below shows the plot of data points on the boxplot by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
stat_summary()
This adds mean values by using stat_summary() and overrides the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom()
This adds mean values by using geom_() and overrides the default stats.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
text:
Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
The code below shows geom_smooth() being used to plot a best-fit curve in the scatterplot. The default method used is loess

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_wrap()
This code wraps a 1-D sequence of panels into 2-D. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_grid()
This code forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Working with Coordinates

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
coord_flip()
Flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Changing the range of x & y-axis
text:
Changing the range of x & y-axis
As the scatterplot below is slightly misleading due to the axis range not being equal,

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









the code below fixes both axis range from 0 to 100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
